relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: YHWH speaks voluntary consecration and blessing.
A man or woman may take a Nazirite vow to YHWH.
Wine, strong drink, vinegar, juice, and grapes are set aside for the vow.
The whole fruit of the vine remains outside the consecrated period.
Uncut hair becomes the visible sign of the vow.
The Nazirite avoids corpse contact during the consecration.
Even close family death does not erase the ritual boundary.
All days of the vow are holy to YHWH.
Unexpected death nearby interrupts the vow without making the Nazirite morally guilty.
Bird offerings mark the return to ritual cleanness.
Covering is made for the contact with death, and the head is reconsecrated.
The days begin again after accidental interruption.
The completed vow comes to the tent entrance.
Burnt, purification, and peace offerings are brought.
Bread, oil, grain, and drink offerings accompany completion.
The priest brings the offerings before YHWH.
The peace/well-being offering crowns the completed vow.
The consecrated hair is shaved and placed beneath the peace offering.
Priestly portions are placed in the Nazirite's hands.
The offerings are waved, and ordinary wine may be received again.
The vow is fulfilled according to promise and what the hand can reach.
YHWH speaks the blessing that will rest on Israel.
Aaron and his sons are taught how to bless the people.
May YHWH bless you and guard you.
May YHWH's face shine toward you with grace.
May YHWH turn his face toward you and give shalom.
The priests place the Name upon Israel, and YHWH gives the blessing.

contemplative_note: Numbers 6 moves from chosen restraint back into ordinary life and then into one of Torah's gentlest gifts: blessing, shining presence, grace, and shalom. Consecration is not self-erasure; it returns the person to life under the Divine face.
